title: Understanding the Problem
text: To find the range of the soccer ball on the Moon, we need to understand how gravity affects the range of a projectile. The range of a projectile is given by the formula: \( R=\frac{v**2\sin(2\theta)}{g} \), where \( v \) is the initial velocity, \( $$\theta$$ \) is the angle of projection, and \( g \) is the acceleration due to gravity.

title: Gravity on the Moon
text: The gravity on the Moon is one-sixth that of Earth's gravity. Earth's gravity is approximately $$9.8$$ m/s², so the Moon's gravity is \( $$\frac{9.8}{6$$} \approx $$1.63$$ \) m/s².

title: Calculate the Range on Mars
text: First, calculate the range of the soccer ball on Mars using the given gravity of $$3.7$$ m/s². Use the formula: \( R=\frac{v**2\sin(2\theta)}{g} \).

title: Range on Mars
text: Calculate the range of the soccer ball on Mars using the formula \( R=\frac{v**2\sin(2\theta)}{g} \) with \( $$v=15$$ \) $$\frac{m}{s}$$, \( $$\theta=45$$ \) degrees, and \( $$g=3.7$$ \) m/s².

title: Calculate the Range on the Moon
text: Now, calculate the range of the soccer ball on the Moon using the same formula, but with the Moon's gravity of $$1.63$$ m/s².

title: Range on the Moon
text: Calculate the range of the soccer ball on the Moon using the formula \( R=\frac{v**2\sin(2\theta)}{g} \) with \( $$v=15$$ \) $$\frac{m}{s}$$, \( $$\theta=45$$ \) degrees, and \( $$g=1.63$$ \) m/s².
